title: Quiz: Ring-LWE and Module-LWE
description: Test your understanding of structured LWE variants and their efficiency properties

questions.question: What is the polynomial ring R_q = Z_q[x]/(x^n + 1)?

options.text: Polynomials of degree < n with coefficients mod q, with multiplication mod (x^n + 1)
The set of all prime numbers less than q

questions.explanation: R_q consists of polynomials of degree at most n-1 with coefficients in Z_q. When multiplying, we reduce modulo both q (for coefficients) and x^n + 1 (for degree), creating a ring of exactly q^n elements.

questions.question: Why is the polynomial x^n + 1 chosen as the modulus in Ring-LWE when n is a power of 2?

options.text: It's the only polynomial that works
It's irreducible over Q and enables efficient NTT operations

questions.explanation: x^n + 1 (cyclotomic polynomial) is chosen because: (1) it's irreducible over Q, enabling security reductions, (2) for n = 2^k, it splits nicely mod certain primes, enabling NTT for fast O(n log n) multiplication, and (3) it creates useful negacyclic structure.

questions.question: What is the main efficiency advantage of Ring-LWE over standard LWE?

options.text: Ring-LWE has O(n) key sizes vs O(n²) for standard LWE
Ring-LWE doesn't require a modulus q

questions.explanation: In standard LWE, the public key includes an m×n matrix (O(n²) elements). In Ring-LWE, a single ring element encodes all this structure in just n coefficients (O(n)). This reduces key sizes by a factor of n.

questions.question: What does the Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) enable?

options.text: O(n log n) polynomial multiplication instead of O(n²)

questions.explanation: NTT transforms polynomials so that multiplication becomes pointwise coefficient multiplication. Forward NTT + pointwise multiply + inverse NTT gives O(n log n) multiplication instead of O(n²) schoolbook multiplication.

questions.question: How does Module-LWE relate to Ring-LWE and standard LWE?

options.text: Module-LWE is a completely different problem
Module-LWE generalizes both: it uses k×k matrices of ring elements

questions.explanation: Module-LWE works with k×k matrices where each entry is a ring element. When k=1, it reduces to Ring-LWE. When n=1 (trivial ring), it reduces to standard LWE. Module-LWE provides a spectrum between these extremes.

questions.question: Why did Kyber choose Module-LWE instead of Ring-LWE?

options.text: Module-LWE provides better security margins with less algebraic structure

questions.explanation: Module-LWE has less algebraic structure than Ring-LWE, making it more resistant to potential algebraic attacks on ideal lattices. The security of Module-LWE is based on module lattices, which are closer to unstructured lattices while still enabling efficient NTT operations.

questions.question: In the ring R_q = Z_q[x]/(x^n + 1), what is x^n equivalent to?
questions.explanation: Since we reduce modulo x^n + 1, we have x^n + 1 ≡ 0, which means x^n ≡ -1. This creates 'negacyclic' structure: when a product has degree ≥ n, the high-degree terms wrap around with negation.

questions.question: For Kyber-768 with n=256 and k=3, what is the effective security dimension?
questions.explanation: The effective dimension for Module-LWE is n × k. For Kyber-768, this is 256 × 3 = 768, giving the scheme its name. This larger effective dimension provides stronger security than Ring-LWE with n=256 alone.

questions.question: Standard LWE has stronger security guarantees than Ring-LWE because it has less algebraic structure.
questions.explanation: True. Standard LWE works with unstructured random matrices, while Ring-LWE works with ideal lattices that have algebraic structure. While no practical attacks exploit this structure, the consensus is that less structure = more conservative security. This is why Kyber chose Module-LWE as a middle ground.
